title: Outliers: find them and decide what to do
intro: The register at "Mama John's" sees ordinary receipts — and suddenly an order for twenty pizzas: an office party. One such value can flip the mean, inflate the variance and ruin a model. Yet an outlier is not always "an error": sometimes it is the most valuable thing in the data. Let's learn to find them — including on the box plot — and, crucially, to decide deliberately what to do with them.

beats.text: An outlier is a value that has broken far away from the bulk. The widget shows the same data in three ways at once: points at the bottom (the green mean has drifted toward the outlier, the yellow median stayed with the group); a histogram in the middle (the outlier is a lonely bar far to the right — a long tail); a box plot on top (a red dot flying past the whisker). One extreme moves the mean, but barely touches the median and the box.

Let's look at the box plot more closely — it is a compact five-number summary of the data. The box runs from Q1 (25% of the data below) to Q3 (75% below) and covers the central half of the values; the line inside is the median. The outlier boundary is set by the one-and-a-half interquartile range rule: IQR is the box width (Q3 − Q1); the whiskers reach the farthest values within 1.5·IQR of the box edges, and anything beyond is drawn as a separate dot. Such a dot is not a verdict of "error" but a signal: the value is unusually far from the bulk, take a look.
You found an outlier — now what? First question: is it an error or reality?
beats.predict: A salary of "−5" and a salary of "director, 280" — are these the same kind of case?
beats.reveal: No. "−5" is an obvious input error (salaries below zero do not exist): fix it or drop it. "280" is a real value: deleting it means lying about the data. A real outlier is not "garbage" but a signal — possibly the most interesting part.

beats.text: Cycle through the action buttons and watch how all three views change at once.
beats.predict: Which action pulls the mean back to the main group hardest — capping, winsorizing or removing?
beats.reveal: All three remove the extreme's "pull": the dot past the whisker disappears, the histogram stops stretching into a tail, the scale contracts, and the mean returns to the group. The difference: capping and winsorizing KEEP the observation (clipped to a sensible threshold), while removal throws it away entirely — acceptable only for clear errors. The median and the Q1–Q3 box barely move the whole time — they are robust.

beats.text: The golden rule: never delete outliers just because they are inconvenient. First understand their nature, choose a deliberate action, document it, and check how it affects the conclusion. Quietly dropping inconvenient points is exactly what fitting the data to a desired answer looks like.

definitions.text: A value visibly detached from the bulk of the data.
definitions.simple: a point living apart from the rest of the data.

definitions.text: A compact summary of the data: a box from Q1 to Q3 with a median line inside, whiskers to the edges of ordinary values, and separate dots for outliers. It lets you see the center, spread and stray values in one glance.
definitions.simple: five numbers instead of the whole pile: the box is the central half, whiskers are the edges, dots are outliers.

definitions.text: The outlier boundary: a value farther than 1.5 box-widths (1.5·IQR, where IQR = Q3 − Q1) from Q1 or Q3. It relies on robust quartiles rather than the mean, so it holds even on tailed data.
definitions.simple: farther than one and a half "boxes" from the middle — an outlier candidate.

definitions.text: Clip extreme values to a threshold (e.g. p1/p99) while keeping the observation itself in the data.
definitions.simple: do not throw it out — trim it to an allowed ceiling.

definitions.text: Take the logarithm to compress the long tail and pull the data toward symmetry.
definitions.simple: squeezes huge values so the tail stops driving the analysis.

decision: A decision about an outlier is a decision about its nature: a data error — fix it; a real "big customer" — keep it and change the method, not the data.
practice: Before any analysis, an analyst draws a box plot and a histogram and checks the outliers: a lonely bar in the tail and a dot past the whisker are visible at once. Then separates: input errors get fixed, real extremes get handled deliberately (robust measures, capping, log), and the decision is documented so the analysis stays reproducible.
Sometimes the outlier IS the goal: a fraudulent transaction, a failing server, anomalous demand. Then you do not remove it — you hunt for it; a whole field of anomaly detection is built on this.
realLife: In money and response times outliers are a fact of life, not a rarity: there is always a pair of giant orders or hung requests. That is why medians and percentiles are the default there, and spread is read off the box plot.
In machine learning uncleaned erroneous outliers drag the model toward themselves (especially with squared error), while real ones are, on the contrary, informative; confusing the two is expensive.
assumptions: The 1.5·IQR rule and the |z|>3 threshold are heuristics, not truth: different distributions have different "normal" shares of outliers. On heavily skewed data 1.5·IQR will flag many perfectly normal tail values.
Removing outliers changes conclusions and easily turns into result-fitting. Every removal must be justified by the nature of the data and documented.
